PR.DS-10.title: Data-in-Use Protection
PR.DS-10.objective: Protect the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of data while it is being actively processed in memory, CPU, or application runtime. This prevents data exposure through memory scraping, side-channel attacks, process injection, or unauthorized inter-process access.

keyActivities.activity: Implement secure memory management practices including clearing sensitive data (cryptographic keys, credentials, PII) from memory immediately after use, using secure string libraries, and disabling core dumps on production systems

Deploy process isolation controls including mandatory access control (SELinux, AppArmor), container isolation (namespace separation, seccomp profiles, read-only root filesystems), and hypervisor-level memory isolation for multi-tenant environments
Evaluate and deploy confidential computing technologies (Intel SGX, AMD SEV, ARM TrustZone) for workloads processing highly sensitive data, ensuring data remains encrypted even during processing

Implement application-level input validation, parameterized queries, output encoding, and memory-safe coding practices to prevent injection attacks, buffer overflows, and other vulnerabilities that could expose data in use

maturity.basic: Basic process isolation is provided by the operating system. Sensitive data may persist in memory longer than necessary, container security relies on default configurations, and application-level memory protections are inconsistent.
maturity.mature: Mandatory access controls and hardened container runtimes enforce process isolation. Secure coding standards mandate memory clearing after use, application security testing validates data-in-use protections, and production systems disable memory dumps.
maturity.advanced: Confidential computing enclaves protect the most sensitive workloads. Runtime application self-protection (RASP) monitors for memory-based attacks, hardware-backed memory encryption is deployed, and continuous fuzz testing validates data-in-use integrity across the application portfolio.

PR.DS-11.title: Backup Management and Testing
PR.DS-11.objective: Ensure that backups of critical data and systems are created, protected, maintained, and regularly tested to support recovery from incidents, disasters, and ransomware attacks. This guarantees recoverability within defined recovery time and recovery point objectives.

keyActivities.activity: Implement automated backup schedules aligned with data criticality: continuous or near-real-time replication for tier-1 systems (databases, financial systems), daily incremental for tier-2, and weekly full backups for tier-3, with defined RPO/RTO targets

Maintain immutable, air-gapped backup copies stored in a geographically separate location using the 3-2-1-1-0 rule (3 copies, 2 media types, 1 offsite, 1 immutable, 0 errors), specifically designed to resist ransomware encryption
Encrypt all backup data at rest and in transit using AES-256, with backup encryption keys managed separately from the production key management system to prevent simultaneous compromise

Test backup restoration quarterly for critical systems and annually for all systems, including full bare-metal recovery tests, individual file/database restoration, and cross-site failover validation, documenting results and remediation of failures

Monitor backup job success/failure rates daily, investigate and remediate failures within 24 hours, and report backup health metrics (completion rate, data growth, storage utilization, restoration test results) to leadership monthly

maturity.basic: Backups are configured for some systems but schedules are inconsistent. Restoration testing is rare or undocumented, backup monitoring is reactive, and there are no immutable or air-gapped copies to protect against ransomware.
maturity.mature: Automated backups run on defined schedules aligned with data tiering. Immutable offsite copies exist, encryption is enforced, quarterly restoration tests are completed and documented, and backup health is monitored and reported.
maturity.advanced: Continuous data protection provides near-zero RPO for critical systems. Automated restoration testing runs continuously in isolated environments with success validation, backup integrity is cryptographically verified, and predictive analytics forecast storage needs and identify backup anomalies that could indicate compromise.

PR.PS-01.title: Configuration Management Practices
PR.PS-01.objective: Establish and enforce configuration management practices that ensure all technology platforms operate in a secure, known, and approved state. This prevents security drift, unauthorized changes, and exploitation of misconfigurations.

keyActivities.activity: Develop and maintain hardened baseline configurations for all platform types (servers, workstations, network devices, cloud resources, containers) based on CIS Benchmarks, DISA STIGs, or vendor security guides, enforcing the principle of least functionality

Deploy configuration management and infrastructure-as-code tools (Ansible, Terraform, Puppet, Chef) to automate baseline deployment, prevent configuration drift, and enable rapid remediation of deviations

Implement a formal change management process requiring security impact assessment, testing, approval, and rollback planning for all configuration changes, with emergency change procedures for urgent security updates

Conduct continuous configuration compliance monitoring using tools (Qualys Policy Compliance, Tenable, AWS Config, Azure Policy) that compare running configurations against approved baselines and alert on deviations
Review and update default configuration settings during all software installations and upgrades, documenting deviations from vendor defaults with security justification and approval

maturity.basic: Baseline configurations exist for some platforms but are informally maintained. Configuration changes are made ad-hoc without consistent change management, and compliance monitoring is periodic (manual audits) rather than continuous.
maturity.mature: Hardened baselines based on CIS/STIG are defined for all platforms and deployed via infrastructure-as-code. A formal change management process is followed, continuous compliance monitoring detects drift, and remediation is tracked to completion.
maturity.advanced: Immutable infrastructure patterns (golden images, containerized workloads) prevent drift by design. Self-healing automation remediates detected deviations within minutes, configuration compliance is a real-time metric on security dashboards, and ML identifies high-risk configuration combinations.

PR.PS-02.title: Software Maintenance and Patching
PR.PS-02.objective: Ensure that all software is maintained, patched, replaced, or removed in a timely manner commensurate with risk, minimizing the window of exposure to known vulnerabilities. This includes routine patching, emergency remediation, end-of-life management, and removal of unauthorized software.

keyActivities.activity: Implement a risk-based vulnerability management program with defined SLAs for patching: critical vulnerabilities within 72 hours, high within 14 days, medium within 30 days, and low within 90 days, with exceptions requiring documented risk acceptance from the system owner
Deploy automated patch management tools (WSUS, SCCM, Intune, Ivanti, Automox) for operating systems and applications, with centralized reporting on patch compliance rates across all environments

Maintain a software lifecycle inventory that tracks all installed software versions, end-of-life dates, and support status, with proactive planning (12+ months in advance) for migration off end-of-life products

For containerized workloads, rebuild and redeploy container images with updated base images and dependencies rather than patching running containers, integrated into the CI/CD pipeline with automated vulnerability scanning

Conduct quarterly scans for unauthorized, unsupported, and unnecessary software (including OS utilities that could be abused by attackers), and enforce removal with documented justification for any exceptions

maturity.basic: Patching occurs on an ad-hoc or monthly cycle without risk-based prioritization. End-of-life software remains in use, unauthorized software removal is reactive, and patch compliance is not consistently measured across all environments.
maturity.mature: Risk-based patch SLAs are defined and enforced, automated patch management covers all managed endpoints, end-of-life software has documented migration plans, and monthly reporting tracks patch compliance by criticality and business unit.
maturity.advanced: Predictive vulnerability prioritization (EPSS, exploit intelligence) dynamically adjusts patch urgency. Virtual patching provides immediate protection while permanent patches are tested, automated pipelines rebuild and redeploy containerized workloads within hours of vulnerability disclosure, and patch compliance exceeds 98% across all environments.

PR.PS-03.title: Hardware Maintenance and Lifecycle
PR.PS-03.objective: Ensure that hardware is maintained, replaced, and disposed of commensurate with risk, preventing security gaps from outdated hardware that cannot support current security controls and ensuring sensitive data is not exposed through improper disposal.

keyActivities.activity: Maintain a comprehensive hardware asset inventory with lifecycle tracking (acquisition date, warranty status, firmware version, security capability assessment, projected end-of-life) for all servers, network equipment, endpoints, and IoT devices

Establish hardware replacement criteria based on security capability requirements (e.g., TPM 2.0 support, Secure Boot, firmware update availability) and proactively plan replacement 12 months before hardware can no longer support required security controls

Implement certified data sanitization procedures (NIST SP 800-88 guidelines) for all hardware being decommissioned, including cryptographic erasure for self-encrypting drives, degaussing for magnetic media, and physical destruction for highest-classification devices

Maintain auditable chain-of-custody records for all hardware disposal, including sanitization method, verification, responsible personnel, and certificates of destruction from third-party disposal vendors

maturity.basic: Hardware inventory is maintained in spreadsheets and updated irregularly. Disposal is handled informally without consistent data sanitization procedures, and hardware is replaced reactively when it fails rather than proactively based on security capability gaps.
maturity.mature: A formal hardware lifecycle management program tracks all assets from procurement through disposal. Replacement plans are budgeted based on security capability requirements, NIST SP 800-88 sanitization is followed with certificates of destruction, and chain-of-custody is maintained.
maturity.advanced: Automated asset discovery continuously validates the hardware inventory. Firmware integrity verification detects tampering, supply chain security controls validate hardware authenticity at procurement, and environmental sustainability metrics are integrated into lifecycle decisions alongside security requirements.

PR.PS-04.title: Log Generation and Availability
PR.PS-04.objective: Ensure that all systems, applications, and services generate comprehensive log records and make them available to centralized monitoring infrastructure for continuous analysis. This provides the evidentiary foundation for threat detection, incident investigation, and compliance.

keyActivities.activity: Define an enterprise logging standard specifying required log sources (OS events, application logs, authentication logs, network flows, cloud audit trails), minimum data fields (timestamp, source, user, action, outcome, resource), and retention periods aligned with regulatory requirements

Configure all operating systems, applications, network devices, cloud services, and SaaS platforms to generate security-relevant logs and forward them to the centralized SIEM/log management platform using secure transport (TLS-encrypted syslog, agent-based collection)

Implement log integrity controls including write-once storage, cryptographic hashing, tamper-evident logging, and access controls that prevent log modification or deletion by administrators of the source systems

Monitor log pipeline health (ingestion rates, parsing errors, latency, source connectivity) continuously, with automated alerting when log sources stop sending data or ingestion anomalies are detected
Ensure logs capture the data elements required for zero trust architectures including device identity, posture assessment results, resource access decisions, and policy evaluation outcomes

maturity.basic: Logging is enabled on some systems with default configurations. Log forwarding to a central platform is incomplete, retention is inconsistent, and log pipeline health is not monitored, resulting in gaps during incident investigations.
maturity.mature: An enterprise logging standard is defined and enforced. All critical systems forward logs to the SIEM with secure transport, retention meets regulatory requirements, log integrity controls are in place, and pipeline health is continuously monitored.
maturity.advanced: Universal log coverage across all environments (on-premises, cloud, SaaS, OT) with real-time ingestion. Adaptive log enrichment automatically adds context (threat intel, asset criticality, user risk scores), and log analytics platforms process petabytes of data with sub-second query performance for investigation.

PR.PS-05.title: Unauthorized Software Prevention
PR.PS-05.objective: Prevent the installation and execution of unauthorized, unapproved, or malicious software on organizational systems. This reduces the attack surface by ensuring only known, vetted, and approved software can run.

keyActivities.activity: Implement application whitelisting (allowlisting) on high-risk systems (servers, privileged workstations, POS systems, OT endpoints) using tools such as Windows Defender Application Control, AppLocker, or Carbon Black App Control

Maintain a curated software catalog of approved applications with version requirements, and configure endpoint management tools (Intune, JAMF, SCCM) to allow installation only from the approved catalog or managed app stores

Verify the integrity and provenance of all software before deployment by validating publisher digital signatures, checking package hashes against vendor-published values, and scanning with anti-malware tools

Configure DNS filtering (e.g., Cisco Umbrella, Cloudflare Gateway) to block access to known malicious domains, command-and-control infrastructure, and unauthorized software download sites across all network segments

maturity.basic: Anti-malware software is deployed but software installation is not restricted. Users can install arbitrary applications, and DNS filtering is basic or nonexistent. Unauthorized software is discovered reactively during audits.
maturity.mature: Application whitelisting is enforced on high-risk systems, a managed software catalog restricts installations on standard endpoints, DNS filtering blocks known malicious domains, and regular scans identify unauthorized software for removal.
maturity.advanced: Application whitelisting is universally enforced with automated catalog updates. Zero-trust application verification validates software integrity and behavior at runtime, AI-powered threat detection identifies novel malicious software that bypasses signature-based controls, and software supply chain verification is automated.

PR.PS-06.title: Secure Software Development Lifecycle
PR.PS-06.objective: Integrate security practices throughout the software development lifecycle (SDLC) to produce software with minimal vulnerabilities and protect it from tampering. This ensures that internally developed software meets security requirements before deployment to production.

keyActivities.activity: Implement a secure SDLC framework (NIST SSDF, Microsoft SDL, OWASP SAMM) with defined security gates at each phase: threat modeling during design, static analysis during development, dynamic testing during QA, and security sign-off before release

Integrate automated security testing into CI/CD pipelines: SAST (static analysis), SCA (software composition analysis for open-source dependencies), DAST (dynamic testing), container image scanning, and IaC security scanning, with quality gates that block deployment of high-severity findings

Protect source code repositories and build systems with strict access controls, branch protection rules, signed commits, and build provenance attestation (SLSA framework) to prevent unauthorized code injection and supply chain attacks

Conduct periodic manual security assessments (code review, penetration testing) for high-risk applications, supplementing automated testing with expert analysis of business logic flaws, authentication bypasses, and authorization vulnerabilities

Track and remediate security findings from all testing activities through a centralized vulnerability management system with defined SLAs, and maintain secure disposal procedures for applications being decommissioned

maturity.basic: Security testing is limited to periodic penetration tests before major releases. Developers have minimal secure coding training, SAST/DAST tools are not integrated into the build pipeline, and open-source dependency risks are not systematically managed.
maturity.mature: A formal secure SDLC is adopted with threat modeling, automated SAST/SCA/DAST in CI/CD, quality gates that block vulnerable releases, and repository security controls. Security findings are tracked with defined SLAs and reported to development leadership.
maturity.advanced: Security is fully embedded in the developer workflow with real-time IDE-integrated security feedback, automated fix suggestions, SLSA Level 3+ build provenance, and continuous fuzz testing. Security champions are embedded in each team, vulnerability escape rates are tracked, and the secure SDLC maturity is benchmarked against OWASP SAMM.

PR.IR-01.title: Network Access Protection and Segmentation
PR.IR-01.objective: Protect networks and environments from unauthorized logical access through segmentation, access controls, and zero trust principles. This limits lateral movement, reduces blast radius from compromises, and enforces trust boundaries between network zones.

keyActivities.activity: Design and implement network segmentation architecture with defined trust zones (corporate IT, DMZ, production, development, IoT/OT, guest) using VLANs, firewalls, and software-defined networking, with documented traffic flow policies between zones

Deploy next-generation firewalls (NGFW) at all trust boundaries with application-aware policies, intrusion prevention (IPS), TLS inspection for non-sensitive traffic categories, and deny-by-default rules permitting only documented and approved traffic flows

Implement microsegmentation for critical workloads (databases, PCI environment, crown jewel applications) using host-based firewalls, cloud security groups, or dedicated microsegmentation platforms (Illumio, Guardicore) to limit lateral movement

Deploy network access control (NAC) to verify endpoint identity and security posture (OS patch level, anti-malware status, disk encryption) before granting network access, quarantining non-compliant devices for remediation
Implement zero trust network access (ZTNA) for remote and third-party access, replacing traditional VPN with per-application access based on identity, device posture, and contextual risk signals

maturity.basic: Basic network segmentation exists (corporate vs. DMZ) with flat internal networks. Firewall rules are permissive and poorly documented, VPN provides broad network access to remote users, and endpoint posture is not verified before network admission.
maturity.mature: Multi-tier segmentation is implemented with documented trust zones and traffic flow policies. NGFW with IPS protects all boundaries, NAC verifies endpoint posture, microsegmentation protects critical workloads, and firewall rules are reviewed semi-annually.
maturity.advanced: Zero trust architecture with ZTNA provides per-application access. Software-defined microsegmentation dynamically adjusts based on threat intelligence and behavioral analytics, encrypted traffic inspection covers all non-exempt flows, and network access decisions are continuously re-evaluated based on real-time risk signals.

PR.IR-02.title: Environmental Threat Protection
PR.IR-02.objective: Protect organizational technology assets from environmental threats including fire, flood, extreme temperatures, humidity, and power disruptions. This ensures physical infrastructure resilience and prevents environmental conditions from causing data loss or service outages.

keyActivities.activity: Deploy environmental monitoring systems (temperature, humidity, water leak detection, smoke/fire detection) in all data centers, server rooms, and network closets with automated alerting to facilities and IT operations teams
Implement fire suppression systems (clean agent, pre-action sprinkler) in data centers and server rooms, with regular inspection and testing per local fire codes and insurance requirements

Ensure adequate power infrastructure including uninterruptible power supplies (UPS), backup generators with automatic transfer switches, and power distribution units (PDU) with monitoring, tested under load annually

Include environmental protection requirements and SLA commitments in contracts with colocation providers, cloud service providers, and managed hosting vendors, with annual review of provider compliance certifications (SOC 2, ISO 27001)

maturity.basic: Basic fire suppression and UPS exist in the primary data center. Environmental monitoring is limited, backup generators may not be regularly tested, and service provider environmental controls are not formally assessed.
maturity.mature: Comprehensive environmental protection is deployed across all facilities with redundant controls. Environmental monitoring provides real-time alerting, generators are tested under load annually, and service provider environmental SLAs are contractually defined and verified.
maturity.advanced: Predictive environmental monitoring uses sensor data and weather forecasting to proactively adjust cooling, power, and protection measures. Automated facility management optimizes energy efficiency while maintaining environmental controls, and digital twin simulations model failure scenarios for continuous resilience improvement.

PR.IR-03.title: Resilience and High Availability
PR.IR-03.objective: Implement mechanisms that achieve resilience requirements in both normal and adverse situations, ensuring critical services continue to operate through component failures, attacks, and disasters. This includes eliminating single points of failure and providing graceful degradation.

keyActivities.activity: Conduct a business impact analysis (BIA) to identify critical services and their resilience requirements (RTO, RPO, MTPD), and design architectures that meet these requirements through redundancy, failover, and geographic distribution

Implement redundancy at all infrastructure layers: redundant power supplies, RAID storage, multi-path networking, clustered databases, load-balanced application tiers, and multi-region cloud deployments for tier-1 services

Deploy load balancing and auto-scaling across application and infrastructure tiers to distribute traffic, absorb demand spikes, and automatically replace failed instances, with health checks triggering automatic failover

Conduct regular resilience testing including failover drills, disaster recovery exercises, chaos engineering experiments, and load testing to validate that resilience mechanisms work as designed under realistic failure conditions

Design and implement graceful degradation patterns so that when components fail, services continue to operate with reduced functionality rather than complete outage, and users are transparently notified of degraded service

maturity.basic: Some redundancy exists at the infrastructure level (RAID, dual power supplies) but single points of failure persist in application and network layers. Failover is manual, and disaster recovery testing is infrequent.
maturity.mature: Redundancy is implemented at all layers for critical systems. Load balancing and auto-scaling are operational, automated failover is configured and tested quarterly, disaster recovery exercises validate RTO/RPO targets, and BIA is updated annually.
maturity.advanced: Chaos engineering is practiced regularly to discover hidden failure modes. Multi-region active-active architectures provide continuous availability, automated runbooks handle failure scenarios without human intervention, and resilience metrics (availability, failover time, recovery success rate) are continuously tracked against SLOs.

PR.IR-04.title: Resource Capacity Management
PR.IR-04.objective: Monitor and manage resource capacity (compute, storage, network, power) to ensure adequate availability for current operations and anticipated future demands. This prevents service degradation or outages caused by resource exhaustion.

keyActivities.activity: Implement comprehensive capacity monitoring across all infrastructure: CPU, memory, disk IOPS, storage utilization, network bandwidth, connection pools, and power consumption, with dashboards and automated alerting at defined thresholds (warning at 70%, critical at 85%)

Conduct quarterly capacity planning reviews analyzing growth trends, upcoming projects, seasonal patterns, and business forecasts to project resource needs 6-12 months ahead, with budget requests for capacity additions

Implement auto-scaling policies for cloud and virtualized workloads that dynamically add and remove resources based on demand, with cost guardrails to prevent runaway scaling from anomalous traffic or DDoS attacks

Maintain capacity reserves (hot standby, burst capacity, cloud burst options) for critical systems sufficient to handle expected peak loads plus a defined margin (typically 20-30%) to accommodate unexpected demand surges or DDoS mitigation

maturity.basic: Capacity monitoring is limited to basic system metrics on individual hosts. Capacity planning is reactive, responding to outages or performance complaints rather than forecasting needs, and there is no systematic approach to resource scaling.
maturity.mature: Centralized capacity monitoring with defined thresholds and alerting covers all critical infrastructure. Quarterly capacity reviews drive proactive procurement and scaling decisions, auto-scaling is configured for cloud workloads, and capacity metrics are included in operational dashboards.
maturity.advanced: AI-driven capacity forecasting predicts resource needs based on historical patterns, business growth models, and real-time demand signals. Autonomous scaling optimizes cost and performance simultaneously, capacity risk is modeled as part of enterprise risk management, and FinOps practices ensure efficient resource utilization.

DE.CM-01.title: Network Monitoring and Threat Detection
DE.CM-01.objective: Continuously monitor networks and network services to detect potentially adverse events including unauthorized access, anomalous traffic patterns, and indicators of compromise. This provides early warning of network-based attacks and enables rapid containment.

keyActivities.activity: Deploy network detection and response (NDR) sensors (e.g., Darktrace, ExtraHop, Vectra, Corelight) at key network vantage points (internet egress, inter-zone boundaries, data center core) to detect anomalous traffic, lateral movement, C2 communications, and data exfiltration
Monitor DNS query logs for indicators of compromise (DGA domains, DNS tunneling, lookups to known malicious domains) using DNS security analytics tools integrated with threat intelligence feeds

Collect and analyze NetFlow/IPFIX data to establish network traffic baselines and detect deviations indicative of reconnaissance, lateral movement, data staging, or exfiltration, with automated alerting for threshold violations

Monitor for unauthorized wireless access points (rogue APs) and unauthorized device connections through wireless intrusion detection systems (WIDS), 802.1X enforcement, and periodic wireless surveys of facilities

Implement network traffic analysis for zero trust architecture, continuously evaluating device behavior and communication patterns against expected baselines, with policy enforcement triggered by anomalous changes in security posture

maturity.basic: Network monitoring is limited to SNMP-based availability monitoring and basic IDS signatures at the perimeter. DNS monitoring is not implemented, network flow data is not collected, and rogue wireless detection is limited to periodic manual surveys.
maturity.mature: NDR is deployed at critical network points with behavioral analytics. DNS security monitoring detects tunneling and DGA activity, NetFlow baselines detect anomalous traffic patterns, WIDS monitors for rogue APs, and network alerts are correlated in the SIEM.
maturity.advanced: AI-powered NDR provides real-time behavioral analysis across all network segments including encrypted traffic. Automated response orchestration can isolate compromised segments within seconds, network digital twins enable threat simulation, and continuous threat hunting leverages full-packet capture for retrospective analysis.

DE.CM-02.title: Physical Environment Monitoring
DE.CM-02.objective: Monitor the physical environment to detect potentially adverse events such as unauthorized access, tailgating, tampering with physical controls, and environmental anomalies. This provides early detection of physical security incidents that could lead to or accompany cyber incidents.

keyActivities.activity: Integrate physical access control system (PACS) logs with the SIEM to detect anomalous badge activity (access outside business hours, rapid badge-in at multiple locations, failed attempts, tailgating patterns) with automated alerting and investigation workflows

Deploy and monitor video surveillance systems with recording and retention capabilities at all facility entry points, data center areas, and sensitive zones, with tamper detection on camera systems themselves
Implement tamper detection on physical access controls (door sensors, lock status monitors, hinge pin alarms) with real-time alerting to security operations when tampering is detected

Conduct regular review and audit of physical access logs and visitor records to identify patterns of unauthorized access, policy violations, or social engineering indicators, with findings reported to security management

maturity.basic: Basic badge reader systems log access but logs are only reviewed reactively after incidents. Camera footage is recorded but not actively monitored, and tamper detection on physical controls is limited.
maturity.mature: PACS logs are integrated with the SIEM for automated anomaly detection. Video surveillance is actively monitored during off-hours with tamper-evident cameras, physical access reviews are conducted weekly, and visitor records are audited monthly.
maturity.advanced: AI-powered video analytics automatically detect tailgating, unauthorized access, and anomalous behavior in real-time. Converged physical-cyber SOC correlates badge events with logical access for comprehensive threat detection, and predictive models identify insider threat patterns across physical and digital activity.

DE.CM-03.title: Personnel Activity and Insider Threat Monitoring
DE.CM-03.objective: Monitor personnel activity and technology usage to detect potentially adverse events including insider threats, account compromise, policy violations, and anomalous behavior. This detects threats that originate from or exploit authorized user access.

keyActivities.activity: Deploy user and entity behavior analytics (UEBA) integrated with the SIEM to establish behavioral baselines and detect anomalies such as unusual access patterns, off-hours activity, large data transfers, privilege escalation, and geographic impossibility

Monitor authentication logs across all systems for indicators of credential compromise including brute force attempts, credential stuffing, password spraying, anomalous login locations, and concurrent sessions from different geographies

Deploy deception technology (honeypots, honeyfiles, honey credentials, canary tokens) throughout the network and monitor for any interaction, which indicates unauthorized exploration or compromised credentials

Monitor for data loss indicators including unusual volume of downloads, email attachments to personal accounts, USB transfers, cloud storage uploads, and printing of sensitive documents, with DLP alerting integrated into SIEM workflows

Implement a formal insider threat program with cross-functional participation (security, HR, legal, management) that defines insider threat indicators, investigation procedures, and privacy-compliant monitoring boundaries

maturity.basic: Authentication failure monitoring is limited to basic lockout policies. User activity monitoring is ad-hoc, insider threat detection relies on manager observation, and deception technology is not deployed.
maturity.mature: UEBA is deployed and integrated with the SIEM to detect behavioral anomalies. Authentication monitoring covers all systems, a formal insider threat program is established with defined indicators and investigation procedures, and deception technology is deployed in critical network segments.
maturity.advanced: ML-powered behavioral analytics continuously refine individual risk scores combining activity across all data sources (authentication, DLP, PACS, email, endpoint). Automated risk-adaptive controls dynamically adjust access based on user risk scores, and advanced deception environments create convincing decoy infrastructure that maps attacker techniques to MITRE ATT&CK.

DE.CM-06.title: External Service Provider Monitoring
DE.CM-06.objective: Monitor external service provider activities and services to detect potentially adverse events, ensuring that outsourced services and third-party access do not introduce undetected threats. This extends the security monitoring perimeter to encompass the extended enterprise.

keyActivities.activity: Configure monitoring and logging of all remote administration sessions by external providers (MSPs, outsourced IT, vendor support) including session recording, keystroke logging for privileged access, and real-time alerting for high-risk actions

Ingest and analyze cloud service provider audit logs (AWS CloudTrail, Azure Activity Log, GCP Audit Logs) in the SIEM, with detection rules for anomalous administrative actions, configuration changes, and data access patterns

Establish and monitor service-level baselines for third-party services (availability, response times, error rates) to detect degradation that may indicate compromise, misconfiguration, or denial-of-service affecting service providers

Implement CASB (Cloud Access Security Broker) to monitor SaaS application usage, detect shadow IT, enforce security policies for cloud services, and identify anomalous data access or sharing patterns across sanctioned and unsanctioned cloud services

maturity.basic: Third-party access is logged but not actively monitored. Cloud audit logs are retained but not analyzed in real-time, and there is no systematic approach to detecting anomalous service provider behavior.
maturity.mature: All external provider access is logged and monitored with session recording for privileged access. Cloud audit logs are ingested into the SIEM with active detection rules, CASB monitors SaaS usage, and service baselines detect degradation.
maturity.advanced: Continuous third-party risk monitoring integrates real-time behavioral analysis of provider activity with external threat intelligence on supply chain compromises. Automated playbooks can instantly revoke provider access upon detection of anomalous behavior, and provider security posture is continuously assessed through automated evidence collection.

DE.CM-09.title: Endpoint and Software Monitoring
DE.CM-09.objective: Monitor computing hardware, software, runtime environments, and their data to detect potentially adverse events including malware, unauthorized changes, configuration drift, and signs of compromise. This provides host-level visibility that complements network-based detection.

keyActivities.activity: Deploy endpoint detection and response (EDR) on all endpoints and servers (e.g., CrowdStrike Falcon, Microsoft Defender for Endpoint, SentinelOne) with behavioral detection, memory analysis, and automated containment capabilities, managed by the SOC

Monitor email, web browsing, file sharing, and collaboration platforms for malware delivery, phishing, data leaks, and social engineering using secure email gateways (SEG), web proxies, and CASB with threat intelligence-enriched detection

Implement file integrity monitoring (FIM) on critical systems to detect unauthorized changes to system files, configurations, registry keys, and application binaries, with alerts correlated in the SIEM for investigation

Monitor software configurations continuously against approved security baselines using compliance scanning tools, alerting on drift and unauthorized changes that may indicate compromise or misconfiguration

Deploy endpoint health verification that assesses device posture (OS version, patch level, EDR status, encryption status, firewall state) before granting access, redirecting non-compliant endpoints to remediation environments

maturity.basic: Traditional antivirus is deployed on endpoints with signature-based detection. FIM is limited to critical servers, email filtering relies on basic spam/malware rules, and configuration compliance monitoring is periodic rather than continuous.
maturity.mature: EDR with behavioral detection is deployed across all endpoints and servers. FIM covers all critical systems, email and web security use advanced threat protection with sandboxing, configuration compliance is continuously monitored, and endpoint health gates access decisions.
maturity.advanced: Extended detection and response (XDR) correlates endpoint, email, identity, and cloud telemetry for unified threat detection. Automated investigation and response playbooks handle common threats without analyst intervention, runtime application self-protection (RASP) monitors application behavior, and threat hunting proactively searches for indicators of compromise across all endpoint telemetry.

DE.AE-02.title: Adverse Event Log Analysis
DE.AE-02.objective: Analyze potentially adverse events to better understand associated activities, attack techniques, and scope. This transforms raw security data into actionable intelligence that enables accurate threat characterization and informed response decisions.

keyActivities.activity: Operate a SIEM platform (e.g., Splunk, Microsoft Sentinel, Elastic SIEM, IBM QRadar) with continuously tuned detection rules, correlation searches, and dashboards that monitor for known malicious patterns, TTPs, and anomalous activity across all log sources

Integrate commercial and open-source cyber threat intelligence (CTI) feeds (STIX/TAXII, MISP, vendor feeds) into the SIEM and detection tools to improve detection accuracy, enrich alerts with threat actor context, and identify IoCs in historical logs
Conduct regular manual log reviews for systems and technologies that cannot be sufficiently monitored through automated detection, including OT/SCADA systems, legacy applications, and niche platforms

Develop and maintain a detection engineering program that creates, tests, and refines detection rules mapped to MITRE ATT&CK techniques, with regular testing through adversary emulation and detection gap analysis

Generate and distribute regular analysis reports (daily SOC summaries, weekly trend reports, monthly executive dashboards) that communicate detection findings, emerging patterns, and security posture to technical and leadership stakeholders

maturity.basic: SIEM is deployed with vendor-default rules. Alert triage is reactive and manual, threat intelligence is not integrated, and log analysis is limited to investigating known incidents rather than proactive detection.
maturity.mature: SIEM has custom detection rules mapped to MITRE ATT&CK with regular tuning. CTI feeds enrich alerts, a detection engineering program continuously improves coverage, manual log reviews supplement automated monitoring, and regular reporting communicates findings to stakeholders.
maturity.advanced: ML-augmented analytics detect novel attack patterns and reduce false positives. Purple team exercises continuously validate detection coverage, automated detection-as-code pipelines deploy and test new rules in hours, and retrospective analysis of threat intelligence automatically hunts for previously undetected compromises in historical data.

DE.AE-03.title: Multi-Source Event Correlation
DE.AE-03.objective: Correlate information from multiple sources to provide a comprehensive view of security events, enabling detection of complex attacks that span multiple systems and attack vectors. This transforms siloed alerts into coherent attack narratives.

keyActivities.activity: Centralize log collection from all security-relevant sources (endpoints, network devices, cloud services, identity providers, email gateways, physical access systems) into the SIEM with normalized data schemas and consistent timestamp synchronization (NTP)

Develop and maintain correlation rules that combine signals from multiple data sources to detect multi-stage attacks (e.g., phishing email + credential use from anomalous location + lateral movement + data exfiltration), mapped to MITRE ATT&CK kill chain stages

Integrate cyber threat intelligence into correlation analysis to identify when observed activity across multiple sources matches known threat actor TTPs, campaign indicators, or emerging threat patterns

Implement entity-centric analytics that aggregate all activity associated with a user, device, or service into unified timelines, enabling analysts to quickly understand the full scope of potentially compromised entities

maturity.basic: Logs from multiple sources are collected in the SIEM but analyzed in isolation. Correlation is limited to basic rules (e.g., multiple failed logins), and analysts manually piece together events across different consoles during investigations.
maturity.mature: The SIEM ingests and normalizes logs from all major sources with consistent schemas. Multi-stage correlation rules detect complex attack patterns, entity-centric views aggregate related events, and CTI enrichment connects observed activity to known threats.
maturity.advanced: Graph-based analytics and knowledge graphs automatically identify relationships between entities and events across all data sources. ML models detect previously unknown attack chains, automated investigation playbooks generate comprehensive attack narratives, and real-time streaming correlation processes events at scale with sub-second latency.

DE.AE-04.title: Impact and Scope Estimation
DE.AE-04.objective: Understand the estimated impact and scope of adverse events to inform response prioritization and resource allocation. This ensures that response efforts are proportional to the actual or potential business impact of detected threats.

keyActivities.activity: Define and maintain an impact assessment framework that maps affected assets to business processes, data classification levels, regulatory exposure, and financial impact, enabling rapid estimation of incident severity

Configure SIEM correlation rules to automatically enrich alerts with asset criticality, data classification, business owner, and regulatory context from the CMDB and asset inventory, enabling analysts to quickly estimate business impact

Develop and maintain blast radius analysis capabilities that can rapidly determine the potential scope of a compromise based on network segmentation, trust relationships, credential reuse, and lateral movement paths

Conduct regular tabletop exercises and incident simulations that practice impact and scope estimation for various attack scenarios, refining estimation techniques and ensuring responders can make accurate assessments under pressure

maturity.basic: Impact estimation is informal and relies on individual analyst judgment. Asset criticality information is not readily available during incident triage, and scope estimation requires manual investigation across multiple systems.
maturity.mature: An impact assessment framework links assets to business criticality and regulatory exposure. SIEM alerts are enriched with asset context, blast radius analysis tools are available to responders, and tabletop exercises practice impact estimation for common scenarios.
maturity.advanced: Automated impact scoring dynamically calculates business impact based on real-time asset graph analysis, active attack path modeling, and financial impact quantification. Scope estimation tools automatically map the potential blast radius using network topology, credential graphs, and trust relationships, providing responders with instant situational awareness.

DE.AE-06.title: Adverse Event Information Distribution
DE.AE-06.objective: Provide information on adverse events to authorized staff and tools in a timely manner to enable rapid response and coordinated action. This ensures that the right people and systems receive actionable intelligence when threats are detected.

keyActivities.activity: Configure SIEM and security tools to generate automated alerts through multiple channels (SOC console, email, SMS, PagerDuty/Opsgenie, Slack/Teams) based on event severity and escalation matrix, ensuring 24/7 coverage for critical alerts

Implement automated ticket creation in the ITSM/SOAR platform (ServiceNow, Jira, Splunk SOAR) when security events meet defined thresholds, with automatic assignment to appropriate response teams based on event type, affected assets, and business context

Establish role-based access to security dashboards, investigation platforms, and log analysis tools so that incident responders, IT operations, and authorized managers can access relevant findings at all times without bottlenecks

Define and enforce escalation procedures with timebound escalation thresholds (e.g., critical events escalated to incident commander within 15 minutes, executive notification within 1 hour for severity-1 incidents) and after-hours on-call rotation

maturity.basic: Alerts are generated by individual tools but not centrally managed. Notification relies on email, escalation procedures are informal, and there is no automated ticket creation for security events.
maturity.mature: SIEM generates centralized alerts with automated multi-channel notification based on severity. Tickets are automatically created and assigned, escalation procedures are defined and tested, and responders have self-service access to investigation tools and dashboards.
maturity.advanced: SOAR orchestrates end-to-end alert lifecycle from detection through notification, enrichment, and initial response. Intelligent alert routing adapts based on analyst workload, skillset, and shift coverage, dynamic escalation automatically adjusts based on evolving event severity, and mobile-accessible war room dashboards provide real-time situational awareness during major incidents.

DE.AE-07.title: Threat Intelligence Integration
DE.AE-07.objective: Integrate cyber threat intelligence and other contextual information into the analysis of adverse events to improve detection accuracy, understand threat actor motivations and capabilities, and anticipate follow-on attacks. This transforms raw alerts into contextualized threat assessments.

keyActivities.activity: Subscribe to and operationalize multiple threat intelligence sources (commercial feeds, ISACs/ISAOs, government advisories, open-source feeds like AlienVault OTX, MISP communities) covering strategic, operational, and tactical intelligence relevant to the organization's industry and threat profile

Automate the ingestion of tactical indicators of compromise (IoCs) into detection tools (SIEM, EDR, NGFW, DNS filtering, email gateway) through a threat intelligence platform (TIP) using STIX/TAXII standards, with confidence scoring and automated expiration

Integrate asset inventory, data classification, and vulnerability scan data into the analysis workflow so that analysts can quickly assess whether detected threats target vulnerable assets and determine the potential business impact

Monitor vendor security advisories, CVE disclosures, and third-party vulnerability reports for the organization's technology stack, and rapidly assess applicability and exposure, feeding confirmed threats into detection and response workflows

maturity.basic: Threat intelligence consumption is limited to reading vendor advisories and news articles. IoCs are manually checked against logs during investigations, and no formal threat intelligence platform or feeds are operational.
maturity.mature: A TIP aggregates and normalizes intelligence from multiple feeds. Automated IoC ingestion distributes indicators to detection tools, analysts use CTI to enrich investigations and understand threat actor TTPs, and vulnerability advisories are systematically tracked and assessed.
maturity.advanced: Threat intelligence is fully operationalized across all security functions (detection, hunting, vulnerability management, risk assessment). Automated threat modeling correlates intelligence against the organization's specific attack surface, predictive intelligence anticipates emerging threats, and the organization contributes intelligence back to sharing communities.

DE.AE-08.title: Incident Declaration Criteria
DE.AE-08.objective: Declare incidents when adverse events meet defined incident criteria, ensuring consistent and timely classification of security events that require formal incident response procedures. This prevents both under-reporting (missed incidents) and over-reporting (alert fatigue) through clear, documented criteria.

keyActivities.activity: Define and document incident declaration criteria with clear thresholds for different severity levels (e.g., confirmed malware execution = Severity 2, data exfiltration confirmed = Severity 1, phishing email received but not clicked = Event only), reviewed and updated annually

Train all SOC analysts and incident responders on incident declaration criteria, including how to apply criteria to ambiguous situations, when to escalate for classification decisions, and how known false positive patterns should be handled

Implement automated incident classification rules in the SOAR/SIEM that apply incident criteria to enriched alerts, auto-declaring incidents and initiating response workflows when criteria are clearly met, while flagging borderline cases for analyst review

Conduct monthly reviews of incident declaration accuracy, analyzing false positive rates, missed incidents, classification consistency across analysts, and time-to-declare metrics, with findings driving criteria refinement and analyst training

maturity.basic: Incident declaration relies on individual analyst judgment without formal criteria. Classification is inconsistent across analysts and shifts, known false positives are not documented, and there is no systematic review of declaration accuracy.
maturity.mature: Documented incident criteria define clear thresholds for declaration and severity classification. SOC analysts are trained on criteria application, known false positives are catalogued, and monthly reviews assess classification consistency and accuracy.
maturity.advanced: Automated classification applies incident criteria to enriched alerts with high accuracy. Machine learning assists borderline classification decisions based on historical patterns, real-time feedback loops adjust criteria based on evolving threat landscape, and incident declaration metrics (accuracy, consistency, time-to-declare) are tracked as SOC performance KPIs.

RS.MA-01.title: Execute Incident Response Plan
RS.MA-01.objective: Ensure the incident response plan is promptly activated and executed in a coordinated manner with all relevant internal teams and third-party partners once an incident is formally declared. This includes triggering automated workflows, engaging external IR retainers, and designating clear incident leadership.

keyActivities.activity: Configure SIEM/SOAR platforms to automatically declare incidents based on detection correlation rules and trigger the IR playbook workflow, including automated notification to the incident response team and pre-designated third parties
Designate an Incident Commander for each declared incident who assumes authority over response coordination, resource allocation, and third-party engagement decisions
Activate contracts with external incident response retainers (e.g., CrowdStrike, Mandiant, Secureworks) based on incident severity thresholds defined in the IR plan
Initiate parallel execution of BCP and DRP plans when incident characteristics indicate potential operational disruption to critical business services
Conduct annual tabletop exercises and biannual functional IR drills to validate plan execution readiness, third-party coordination mechanisms, and handoff procedures

maturity.basic: An incident response plan exists in document form but is infrequently tested. Incident declaration is informal and ad-hoc, with third-party engagement handled reactively on a case-by-case basis.
maturity.mature: The IR plan is codified in SOAR playbooks with automated incident declaration, pre-established third-party engagement criteria, and defined Incident Commander rotation. Tabletop exercises are conducted at least annually.
maturity.advanced: Fully orchestrated IR execution with automated playbook triggering, real-time third-party coordination through secure collaboration platforms, dynamic resource allocation based on incident severity scoring, and continuous plan optimization driven by post-incident metrics and threat intelligence.

RS.MA-02.title: Triage and Validate Incident Reports
RS.MA-02.objective: Ensure all incoming incident reports are promptly reviewed, confirmed as legitimate cybersecurity incidents, and validated to warrant formal incident response activities, filtering out false positives and non-security events.

keyActivities.activity: Establish and maintain a standardized triage procedure with defined criteria for confirming whether a reported event constitutes a cybersecurity incident requiring IR activation versus a false positive or IT operational issue

Implement severity estimation criteria (e.g., CVSS-based scoring, data sensitivity classification, system criticality tiers) to assign initial severity ratings during triage within defined SLA timeframes
Correlate incoming incident reports against threat intelligence feeds, IOC databases, and recent alert telemetry to validate the nature and credibility of the reported incident
Maintain and tune SIEM detection rules and alert thresholds quarterly to reduce false positive rates and improve triage accuracy, tracking false positive ratios as a KPI
Document triage decisions, rationale, and severity assessments in the incident management system within 30 minutes of initial report receipt for audit trail purposes

maturity.basic: Triage is performed manually by available SOC analysts with informal criteria. Severity estimation is subjective and inconsistent, with limited documentation of triage decisions.
maturity.mature: Standardized triage playbooks are implemented in the SOAR platform with defined severity matrices, SLA-driven response times, and automated enrichment from threat intelligence feeds. Triage decisions are consistently documented.
maturity.advanced: AI/ML-assisted triage automatically correlates alerts, enriches with contextual threat intelligence, assigns preliminary severity scores, and routes to appropriate responders. False positive rates are tracked and continuously optimized, with triage accuracy metrics feeding back into detection tuning.

RS.MA-03.title: Categorize and Prioritize Incidents
RS.MA-03.objective: Classify confirmed incidents by type and prioritize them based on scope, likely business impact, time sensitivity, and strategic response considerations to ensure optimal allocation of response resources.

keyActivities.activity: Maintain a formal incident categorization taxonomy (e.g., data breach, ransomware, DDoS, insider threat, account compromise, supply chain attack) aligned with regulatory reporting requirements and integrate it into the incident management platform

Apply a risk-based prioritization matrix that weighs data sensitivity, number of affected systems/users, regulatory implications, business process criticality, and threat actor sophistication to assign incident priority levels

Balance response strategy selection between rapid containment/recovery and extended observation/investigation based on threat actor behavior, intelligence value, and business tolerance for ongoing risk
Review and update the incident categorization taxonomy and prioritization criteria annually, incorporating lessons learned from past incidents and emerging threat landscape changes

maturity.basic: Incidents are informally categorized by the responding analyst based on personal experience. Prioritization is reactive and inconsistent, with no formal taxonomy or scoring methodology.
maturity.mature: A defined incident categorization taxonomy and prioritization matrix are embedded in SOAR workflows. Categories map to predefined response playbooks, and prioritization consistently considers business impact and regulatory requirements.
maturity.advanced: Dynamic prioritization leverages real-time threat intelligence, automated asset criticality mapping, and predictive analytics to continuously reassess incident priority as new information emerges. Response strategy selection is data-driven with documented decision frameworks that account for intelligence collection opportunities.

RS.MA-04.title: Escalate Incidents as Needed
RS.MA-04.objective: Ensure incidents are escalated or elevated to appropriate internal leadership, external stakeholders, and regulatory bodies in a timely manner based on predefined escalation criteria and evolving incident characteristics.

keyActivities.activity: Define and maintain escalation matrices with clear thresholds for severity-based escalation to executive leadership, board of directors, legal counsel, regulators, and law enforcement, including maximum time-to-escalate SLAs

Implement automated escalation triggers in the incident management platform that notify designated stakeholders when severity thresholds are met, time-based SLAs are approaching, or incident scope expands
Track and validate the status of all ongoing incidents through a centralized incident dashboard, ensuring accurate real-time status reporting for escalation decision-making
Establish secure communication channels (e.g., out-of-band comms, encrypted messaging) for escalation communications to prevent adversary awareness of response activities
Conduct quarterly reviews of escalation effectiveness by analyzing escalation timeliness, appropriateness, and stakeholder satisfaction from past incidents

maturity.basic: Escalation decisions are made informally by individual responders. Escalation paths are not well-documented, leading to delays and inconsistent stakeholder notification.
maturity.mature: Formal escalation matrices with defined severity thresholds and time-based SLAs are integrated into the SOAR platform. Automated notifications ensure timely escalation, and out-of-band communication channels are pre-established.
maturity.advanced: Intelligent escalation automation dynamically adjusts escalation paths based on real-time incident evolution, stakeholder availability, and organizational context. Escalation effectiveness metrics are continuously tracked, and escalation criteria are refined based on trend analysis and regulatory changes.

RS.MA-05.title: Apply Incident Recovery Criteria
RS.MA-05.objective: Systematically evaluate predefined criteria to determine when an incident has been sufficiently contained and analyzed to safely initiate recovery operations, balancing urgency of restoration against risk of premature recovery.

keyActivities.activity: Define explicit recovery initiation criteria in the IR plan, including required containment confirmation, root cause identification status, eradication verification, and business impact assessment completion

Assess the operational disruption risk of proposed recovery activities against current business operations, coordinating with business unit leaders to identify acceptable maintenance windows and rollback plans

Conduct a formal recovery readiness assessment that evaluates whether all known threat actor footholds have been eliminated, all IOCs have been swept, and backup integrity has been verified before authorizing recovery
Document the recovery initiation decision, including the criteria evaluated, evidence reviewed, and authorization chain, in the incident record for post-incident review and compliance purposes

maturity.basic: Recovery is initiated based on informal judgment by the responding team with no documented criteria. There is limited consideration of whether the threat has been fully contained before recovery begins.
maturity.mature: Formal recovery initiation criteria are documented in the IR plan and consistently applied. A structured recovery readiness checklist is completed before authorization, and the decision is documented with supporting evidence.
maturity.advanced: Recovery initiation decisions are supported by automated threat sweep results, validated containment metrics, and quantitative risk assessments. Decision frameworks dynamically account for threat actor persistence techniques and incorporate threat intelligence on adversary re-entry patterns.

RS.AN-03.title: Analyze Incident Root Cause
RS.AN-03.objective: Conduct thorough technical and contextual analysis to reconstruct the full sequence of events during an incident, identify all involved assets, determine the vulnerabilities exploited, and establish the systemic root cause to inform both immediate response and long-term remediation.

keyActivities.activity: Reconstruct the complete incident timeline by correlating logs from SIEM, EDR, network traffic analysis, authentication systems, and cloud audit trails to determine the precise sequence of attacker actions and lateral movement
Identify the initial attack vector, exploited vulnerabilities, and threat actor TTPs using MITRE ATT&CK framework mapping to build a comprehensive understanding of the attack chain

Perform systemic root cause analysis (e.g., 5 Whys, fault tree analysis) to identify underlying organizational, process, or technical failures that enabled the incident beyond the immediate technical vulnerability
Analyze deception technology artifacts (honeypots, honeytokens, canary files) for additional attacker behavioral intelligence and validate findings against network forensic evidence
Produce a structured incident analysis report documenting findings, root causes, and recommended remediations within defined SLAs based on incident severity

maturity.basic: Root cause analysis is performed informally by available analysts using manual log review. Analysis depth varies by analyst skill, and findings are inconsistently documented.
maturity.mature: Structured root cause analysis methodology is applied consistently, leveraging centralized SIEM correlation, EDR telemetry, and MITRE ATT&CK mapping. Analysis reports follow a standard template and feed into remediation tracking.
maturity.advanced: Automated attack chain reconstruction powered by XDR and AI-driven correlation produces near-real-time timeline visualization. Root cause analysis integrates threat intelligence, asset criticality context, and historical incident patterns to identify systemic weaknesses and predict future attack paths.

RS.AN-06.title: Record Investigation Actions Immutably
RS.AN-06.objective: Ensure all actions performed during incident investigation are thoroughly recorded in an immutable, tamper-evident manner to maintain chain of custody, support legal proceedings, and enable comprehensive post-incident review.

keyActivities.activity: Require all incident responders, system administrators, and security engineers involved in response activities to log every action taken (commands executed, systems accessed, configurations changed) in the incident management system with timestamps and operator identification
Implement immutable logging for all incident response actions using write-once storage, cryptographic hashing, or blockchain-anchored audit trails to prevent tampering with investigation records
Assign the Incident Commander responsibility for maintaining a master incident chronology document that consolidates all responder action logs, decision rationale, and evidence references
Establish chain-of-custody procedures for all digital evidence and investigation records, including documentation of who accessed records, when, and for what purpose
Conduct quarterly audits of investigation record completeness and integrity from past incidents to validate compliance with documentation standards and identify improvement areas

maturity.basic: Investigation actions are recorded inconsistently, often in personal notes or emails. No formal requirements for immutability or chain of custody exist, and records may be incomplete or modifiable.
maturity.mature: All responders are required to log actions in a centralized incident management platform with enforced timestamps. Immutable storage is used for investigation records, and chain-of-custody procedures are documented and followed.
maturity.advanced: Automated action capture from SOAR, EDR, and privileged access management tools supplements manual logging, creating a comprehensive and tamper-proof audit trail. Record integrity is cryptographically verified, and completeness is automatically validated against playbook steps.

RS.AN-07.title: Collect and Preserve Incident Data
RS.AN-07.objective: Systematically collect, preserve, and safeguard all pertinent incident data and metadata using forensically sound procedures that maintain evidence integrity and chain of custody for legal, regulatory, and analytical purposes.

keyActivities.activity: Execute forensic evidence collection procedures including disk imaging, memory capture, network packet captures, and cloud log exports using validated forensic tools with cryptographic hash verification of all collected artifacts

Maintain a comprehensive evidence catalog documenting data source, collection date/time, collector identity, storage location, hash values, and chain-of-custody transfers for every piece of collected evidence

Store collected evidence in secure, access-controlled, and tamper-evident storage (physical write-blockers for hardware, encrypted and access-logged cloud storage for digital evidence) with defined retention periods aligned to legal hold requirements
Ensure volatile evidence (RAM, running processes, network connections) is captured before containment actions that may alter system state, following a defined order-of-volatility collection procedure
Validate forensic tool licenses, calibration, and chain-of-custody documentation annually to ensure evidence admissibility in legal and regulatory proceedings

maturity.basic: Evidence collection is ad-hoc, relying on individual analyst skills. Volatile evidence is frequently lost due to premature containment actions, and chain-of-custody documentation is informal or absent.
maturity.mature: Forensic collection follows documented procedures with validated tools, order-of-volatility awareness, and consistent chain-of-custody tracking. Evidence is stored in secure, access-controlled environments with cryptographic integrity verification.
maturity.advanced: Automated evidence collection pipelines capture and preserve forensic data at scale across on-premises and cloud environments. AI-assisted evidence triage prioritizes collection efforts, and all evidence metadata is centrally cataloged with automated integrity verification and legal hold management.

RS.AN-08.title: Estimate and Validate Incident Magnitude
RS.AN-08.objective: Determine the full scope and magnitude of an incident by systematically searching for indicators of compromise across all potentially affected systems and validating the extent of adversary access, persistence, and data impact.

keyActivities.activity: Conduct enterprise-wide IOC sweeps using EDR, SIEM, and network detection tools to identify all compromised endpoints, accounts, and systems beyond the initially detected scope

Search for evidence of adversary persistence mechanisms (backdoors, scheduled tasks, registry modifications, implanted credentials, rogue accounts) across all systems in the blast radius and adjacent network segments
Assess data exposure by identifying what data stores, databases, file shares, and cloud repositories the adversary accessed, and quantify the volume and sensitivity of potentially exfiltrated data
Continuously reassess incident magnitude as new evidence emerges during investigation, updating severity ratings, stakeholder notifications, and resource allocation accordingly

maturity.basic: Magnitude estimation is limited to initially detected systems with minimal proactive hunting. Scope validation relies on manual checks of individual systems without systematic IOC sweeps.
maturity.mature: Automated IOC sweeps are executed across the full enterprise using EDR and SIEM correlation. Persistence mechanism hunting follows structured checklists, and data exposure assessments are conducted for all incidents involving data access.
maturity.advanced: Real-time attack surface mapping combined with automated IOC hunting and ML-based anomaly detection continuously validates and updates incident magnitude. Threat intelligence is automatically correlated to predict likely lateral movement paths, and data loss quantification is integrated with DLP telemetry.

RS.CO-02.title: Notify Stakeholders of Incidents
RS.CO-02.objective: Ensure all required internal and external stakeholders, including affected customers, business partners, regulators, and law enforcement, are notified of cybersecurity incidents within mandated timeframes and in accordance with contractual and regulatory obligations.

keyActivities.activity: Maintain a comprehensive stakeholder notification matrix that maps incident types and severity levels to specific notification requirements, including regulatory deadlines (e.g., GDPR 72 hours, SEC 4 business days, state breach notification laws), contractual obligations, and contact information

Execute breach notification procedures within mandated timeframes, including drafting customer notification letters, filing regulatory reports, and coordinating with outside counsel for legal review of all external communications

Notify law enforcement agencies (FBI, Secret Service, local CISA office) for incidents meeting defined thresholds, following pre-established reporting channels and preserving evidence per law enforcement guidance
Inform business partners, suppliers, and customers of incidents that may impact shared services, data, or interconnected systems, consistent with contractual incident notification clauses
Maintain pre-approved notification templates for common incident types that have been reviewed by legal, compliance, and communications teams to enable rapid deployment during incidents

maturity.basic: Notification decisions are made ad-hoc during incidents with uncertain awareness of regulatory timelines. No pre-approved templates exist, and notification responsibilities are unclear.
maturity.mature: A formal notification matrix links incident types to regulatory requirements and stakeholder contacts. Pre-approved templates enable rapid notification, and legal counsel is embedded in the notification workflow for all external communications.
maturity.advanced: Automated notification workflows trigger based on incident categorization, automatically identifying applicable regulatory regimes and contractual obligations. Multi-jurisdictional notification requirements are tracked in real time, and notification compliance is audited with automated evidence collection.

RS.CO-03.title: Share Incident Information Strategically
RS.CO-03.objective: Proactively share incident information with designated internal and external stakeholders, including ISACs, senior leadership, HR, suppliers, and sector partners, in a controlled and secure manner consistent with information sharing agreements and operational security requirements.

keyActivities.activity: Share sanitized threat intelligence (observed TTPs, IOCs, attack patterns) with relevant ISACs and sector-specific sharing communities using TLP-designated protocols to support collective defense

Provide regular status updates to senior leadership and the board of directors during major incidents using pre-defined reporting cadences (e.g., every 4 hours for critical incidents) with executive-appropriate summaries
Notify HR and legal immediately when incidents involve suspected malicious insider activity, credential misuse by employees, or policy violations that may require employment action
Coordinate incident information sharing with critical suppliers and partners per contractual information sharing agreements, ensuring appropriate classification and secure transmission channels
Maintain and regularly test secure, out-of-band communication channels for incident information sharing that remain operational even if primary corporate communications infrastructure is compromised

maturity.basic: Information sharing during incidents is reactive and unstructured. Senior leadership updates are inconsistent, and external sharing with ISACs or partners occurs rarely or not at all.
maturity.mature: Pre-defined information sharing protocols specify what information is shared, with whom, through what channels, and at what classification level. Executive reporting cadences are established, and ISAC participation is active.
maturity.advanced: Automated threat intelligence sharing pipelines disseminate sanitized IOCs and TTPs to trusted communities in near-real-time using STIX/TAXII protocols. Bidirectional information sharing enriches internal investigation with community intelligence, and sharing effectiveness is measured through attribution and detection metrics.

RS.MI-01.title: Contain Cybersecurity Incidents
RS.MI-01.objective: Execute containment actions to prevent the expansion of a cybersecurity incident, limit adversary lateral movement, and reduce the blast radius while preserving forensic evidence and maintaining essential business operations where possible.

keyActivities.activity: Deploy automated containment playbooks in SOAR that isolate compromised endpoints via EDR network quarantine, disable compromised accounts, and block malicious IPs/domains at the firewall and DNS level within minutes of confirmed compromise

Implement network segmentation containment strategies including VLAN isolation, micro-segmentation policy enforcement, and network access control changes to restrict adversary movement while maintaining critical business traffic flows

Enable incident responders to manually execute containment actions (account lockouts, firewall rule changes, service shutdowns) with proper authorization and documentation when automated containment is insufficient or inappropriate
Coordinate with managed security service providers and ISPs to execute upstream containment actions such as null-routing DDoS traffic, blocking C2 domains, or isolating compromised cloud workloads

Maintain and test pre-built containment action packages for common incident types (ransomware, data exfiltration, account compromise, DDoS) to enable rapid deployment with known business impact profiles

maturity.basic: Containment is performed manually and reactively, often with significant delays. Containment actions may inadvertently destroy forensic evidence, and business impact of containment is not assessed beforehand.
maturity.mature: Automated containment playbooks in SOAR execute predefined isolation actions within SLA timeframes. Containment strategies are pre-defined per incident type, evidence preservation is considered, and business impact is assessed before executing disruptive containment measures.
maturity.advanced: Intelligent containment orchestration dynamically selects optimal containment strategies based on real-time threat context, asset criticality, and business impact modeling. Containment effectiveness is measured through adversary dwell-time metrics, and containment actions are automatically validated to confirm successful isolation.

RS.MI-02.title: Eradicate Incident Threats
RS.MI-02.objective: Systematically remove all threat actor artifacts, malware, persistence mechanisms, and unauthorized access from affected systems to eliminate the adversary's presence and prevent re-compromise before recovery operations begin.

keyActivities.activity: Execute malware removal, unauthorized account deletion, backdoor elimination, and persistence mechanism cleanup across all identified compromised systems using validated eradication procedures and verified IOC lists

Reset all potentially compromised credentials including service accounts, API keys, certificates, and tokens across the entire scope of affected systems and any systems where credential reuse is suspected
Patch or mitigate the vulnerabilities exploited during the incident, including both the initial access vector and any additional vulnerabilities leveraged for privilege escalation or lateral movement
Verify eradication completeness by conducting post-eradication IOC sweeps, reviewing system integrity baselines, and monitoring for any signs of adversary re-entry or dormant persistence
Coordinate with third-party managed security service providers for eradication support on complex incidents involving advanced persistent threats, supply chain compromises, or firmware-level implants

maturity.basic: Eradication is performed ad-hoc, often limited to reimaging individual systems without comprehensive persistence mechanism removal. Credential resets are inconsistent, and eradication verification is minimal.
maturity.mature: Structured eradication procedures address malware removal, credential reset, vulnerability patching, and persistence mechanism elimination comprehensively. Post-eradication verification sweeps confirm completeness before recovery authorization.
maturity.advanced: Automated eradication workflows orchestrate cross-platform cleanup actions at enterprise scale, with real-time verification of eradication completeness. Threat intelligence-driven persistence hunting proactively identifies novel persistence techniques, and eradication effectiveness is validated through controlled re-testing and continuous monitoring.
